coredata.dc:title: Preconcentration and extraction of lead ions in vegetable and water samples by N-doped carbon quantum dot conjugated with Fe3O4 as a green and facial adsorbent
coredata.dc:creator: Mashkani, Masoumeh
Mehdinia, Ali
Jabbari, Ali
Bide, Yasamin
Nabid, Mohammad Reza
coredata.dc:description: Magnetically N-doped Carbon quantum dots has been synthesized via a simple chemical method and applied as a sorbent for the preconcentration and extraction of trace amounts of Pb2+ from water and vegetable samples followed by flame atomic absorption spectrometric detection. The nanoparticles were characterized by X-ray diffraction, UV–vis spectra, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, vibrating sample magnetometer analysis and transmission electron microscopy. A central-composite design was used to find the optimum conditions for the preconcentration procedure through response surface methodology. The effects of various parameters such as the pH value, adsorption time, amount of adsorbent, desorption conditions (type, concentration and volume of the eluent and desorption time), sample volume and interfering ions have been studied. Under the optimized conditions, the calibration graph was linear in the range of 0.3–300μgL−1 (R2 =9992). The detection limit and pre-concentration factor were found to be 0.082μgL−1 and 265, respectively.
coredata.dcterms:subject: N-carbon quantum dot
Magnetic solid phase extraction
Lead ions
Central-composite design

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Optimization of effective parameters using central composite design

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The XRD patterns of N-CQDs (a) and N-CQDs after funcionalization of Fe3O4 particles.
FTIR spectrum of Fe3O4 (a) and Fe3O4/N-doped Carbon quantum dots (b).
TEM image of the Fe3O4/N-doped Carbon quantum dots.
Comparison of the extraction efficiency of lead ions using Fe3O4, Fe3O4/C, Fe3O4/N-CQDs sorbents.
Comparison of the performance data for determination of Pb2+ using different analytical techniques.

entry.cross-ref: Wang, Gao, Zang, Li, and Ma (2012)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The nanocomposite recoveries (%) obtained from real samples spiked with Pb2+.

head.ce:title: Preconcentration and extraction of lead ions in vegetable and water samples by N-doped carbon quantum dot conjugated with Fe3O4 as a green and facial adsorbent

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, K. N. Toosi University of Technology, Tehran, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: K. N. Toosi University of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Marine Living Science, Ocean Science Research Center, Iranian National Institute for Oceanography and Atmospheric Science, Tehran, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Iranian National Institute for Oceanography and Atmospheric Science

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Shahid Beheshti University, Tehran, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Magnetically N-doped Carbon quantum dots has been synthesized via a simple chemical method and applied as a sorbent for the preconcentration and extraction of trace amounts of Pb2+ from water and vegetable samples followed by flame atomic absorption spectrometric detection. The nanoparticles were characterized by X-ray diffraction, UV–vis spectra, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, vibrating sample magnetometer analysis and transmission electron microscopy. A central-composite design was used to find the optimum conditions for the preconcentration procedure through response surface methodology. The effects of various parameters such as the pH value, adsorption time, amount of adsorbent, desorption conditions (type, concentration and volume of the eluent and desorption time), sample volume and interfering ions have been studied. Under the optimized conditions, the calibration graph was linear in the range of 0.3–300μgL−1 (R2 =9992). The detection limit and pre-concentration factor were found to be 0.082μgL−1 and 265, respectively.

ce:section.ce:para: As is well known, lead poses severe risks to human health with toxic effects on living organism (Li, Guo, Zhai, & Wang, 2009). Lead constitutes the highest environmental hazard due to its extensive use, toxicity and widespread distribution. Heavy metals, such as lead, are non-degradable and so they can accumulate in the environment (Dadfarnia, Talebi, Shabani, & Beni, 2007). Sensitive and accurate determination of heavy metal ions at trace level is one of the important points of analytical chemistry (Xiang, Huang, & Luo, 2009) The direct determination of these elements in real samples is a difficult task because of the low concentration of metals, which are near or below detection limit and also influence of matrix ions in the analyzed samples (Huang & Hu, 2008). Among the various preconcentration techniques, solid phase extraction (SPE) has been widely used for the determination of trace metal ions in aqueous samples prior to their determination, because of its simplicity, high pre-concentration factor (PF), short time extraction, high recovery, and low consumption of organic solvent. Therefore, it can combine with the different detection techniques (Bagheri, Afkhami, Saber-Tehrani, & Khoshsafar, 2012; Chalapathi & Maddaiah, 2010).

Nowadays, investigation for finding new materials as solid phase extractor is an important issue to preconcentrate transition metals at trace levels (Parham, Pourreza, & Rahbar, 2009). In recent years, carbon-based nanomaterials have attracted more attention due to their unique properties such as their outstanding water dispersibility, high sensitivity and selectivity to the target analytes, high stability, low toxicity, environmental friendliness, favorable biocompatibility, excellent photostability and simple synthetic routes (Dong, Li, Zhou, Wang, Chi, & Chen, 2013, Ju & Chen, 2014). Carbon dots (CDs) are a new member of carbon nanomaterial with sizes below 10nm, which were first obtained during purification of single-walled carbon nanotubes through preparative electrophoresis (Xu et al., 2004). Because of the presence of oxygen containing groups, such as hydroxyl, epoxy, carbonyl, and carboxyl groups on the Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) surface and the very small size and high surface-to-volume ratio and then, high adsorption capacity, they can be useful for using in the separation science. Moreover, surface functionalization also modifies their physical properties, like their solubility in aqueous and non-aqueous solvents (Frigerio et al., 2012; Lim, Shen, & Gao, 2015). Their excellent dispersing capability in aqueous solutions due the presence of many polar moieties (hydroxy, epoxy, and carboxy groups) makes them suitable for the extraction applications (Wei et al., 2015). Little works has been done on the application of quantum dots in the extraction and preconcentration. Magnetic graphene quantum dots have been used as an adsorbent for determination of bisphenol A in water samples (Mohammad-Rezaei, Razmi, Abdollahi, & Matin, 2014). Decoration of titanium oxide nanotubes with CuFe2O4 quantum dots was also performed for solid phase extraction of uranium (Mortada, Moustafa, Ismail, Hassanien, & Aboud, 2015). In the present study, the Fe3O4/N-Carbon quantum dots was synthesized and employed for the preconcentration of Pb2+ from water and vegetable samples using the magnetic solid phase extraction (MSPE) method prior to the determination by FAAS.

Citric acid monohydrate, dicyandiamide, Iron (III) chloride hexahydrate (FeCl3·6H2O), Iron (II) chloride tetrahydrate (FeCl2·4H2O), lead (II) nitrate, ammonia solution (25%), sodium hydroxide and suprapur® nitric acid (HNO3, 65%) were purchased from Merck (Darmstadt, Germany). The double distilled water was used in the preparation of all the solutions, and other reagents used in this study were of analytical grade.

Determination of lead was carried out on an atomic absorption spectrometry (AAS, GBC 932 AA, Victoria, Australia). A digital pH meter, Metrohm 827 (Titrino, Metrohm, Switzerland) was used for pH adjustments. X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) data were collected on an XD-3A diffractometer using Cu Kα radiation. Ultraviolet–Visible (UV–Vis) spectra were obtained using a Shimadzu UV-2100 spectrophotometer. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra (4000–200cm−1) in KBr were recorded using ABB Bomem MB102 (ABB Bomem, Quebec, PQ, Canada). The morphology of the products was investigated by Zeiss – EM10C – 80kV transmission electron microscope (Germany). The magnetic properties of the bare Fe3O4 and the prepared nanocomposite were analyzed using a vibrating sample magnetometer (VSM) (Daghigh Kavir Co., Kashan, Iran) operating at room temperature with applied fields up to 10kOe. The zeta potentials were measured using a Malvern Nano ZS90 Zetasizer Nanoseries system (Malvern Instruments, Malvern, UK).

0.21g (1mmol) citric acid and 0.25g (3mmol) dicyandiamide were dissolved into 5mL water, and stirred to form a clear solution. Then the solution was transferred into a 20mL Teflon lined stainless autoclave. The sealed autoclave was heated to 160°C in an electric oven and kept for additional 4h. The final product was collected by adding ethanol into the solution and centrifuged at 5000rpm for 5min. The solid can be easily re-dispersed into water.

Fe3O4/N-Carbon quantum dots were synthesized through a one-pot synthesis method according to the reported literature (Mohammad-Rezaei et al., 2014) with some modification. Firstly, N-Carbon quantum dots (0.1g) were dispersed in 150mL deionized water by sonication for 10min. Then, 1.214g FeCl3·6H2O were added to N-CQDs solution at room temperature under a nitrogen flow with vigorous stirring. Then, temperature was increased up to 80°C, and 0.485g FeCl2·4H2O was added slowly to the solution containing Fe3+/N-CQDs, which was vigorously stirred for an additional time of 30min. Finally, the ammonia solution was added dropwise to adjust the pH of the solution to 10 for the synthesis of magnetite Fe3O4/N-CQDs. Then, the mixture was allowed to react for 30min under the well-mixed conditions. The black precipitate was magnetically separated and washed several times with distilled water and then dried at room temperature.

Extraction procedure of metal ions was performed by a batch system. Firstly, 25.1mg Fe3O4/N-CQDs sorbents were placed in a beaker, then 400mL sample spiked with 200µgL−1 concentration level of lead was added and its pH was adjusted to 6.5 (by addition of dilute HNO3 or NaOH). The mixture was stirred for 10min to make the sorbents disperse uniformly in the solution. Subsequently, a magnet was deposited at the bottom of the beaker to separate the sorbents from the solution and the magnetic adsorbents were separated from the solution with a strong super magnet with 1.4T magnetic field (1×3×5cm), and washed two times with deionized water. Finally, the adsorbed lead ions were eluted from the sorbents with 1.5mL of 0.5molL−1 HNO3 solution by stirring for 9min. Then, the elution was collected and lead concentration measurements were carried out using FAAS.

The extraction recovery (ER) was calculated according to the following equation:
ER
% =
C
f
V
f
C
i
V
i
× 100
where Cf is the final concentration of analyte in desorption solvent, Ci is the initial concentration of analyte in water sample, and Vf and Vi are the final volume of desorption solution and the volume of water sample, respectively (Rezazadeh, Yamini, Seidi, & Esrafili, 2013).

The relative recovery (RR) is calculated by the following equation:
RR
% =
C
found
-
C
real
C
add
× 100
Cfound represents the concentration of the analyte after adding a known amount of standard to the real sample, Creal is the concentration of the analyte in real sample, and Cadd refers to the concentration of a known amount of standard that was spiked in the real sample (Ziaei, Mehdinia, & Jabbari, 2014).

In conventional analytical studies, univariate optimization requires a long time and not even takes into account the possible factor interactions in experimental studies. Therefore, experimental design is a kind of chemometric approach, which allows simultaneous variation of all experimental factors with interactions, reduces the required time and number of trials which results in the reduction of the overall required costs (Ata, Berber, Çabuk, & Akyüz, 2015; Taghizadeh et al., 2014). In the present study, based on the preliminary experiments, the affecting parameters on adsorption and desorption steps were optimized by a central composite design (CCD) through response surface methodology.

CCD, as a popular second order experimental design, was used to design the experiment. The CCD is an effective design that is used for sequential experimentation and provides a reasonable amount of information for testing well-fitting and does not require an unusually large number of design points, thereby reduces the overall cost associated with the experiment. In this study, Face-centered CCD (FCCCD) was employed to optimize the parameters. FCCCD is a type of CCD, in which it is considered that |α|=1 (α=star point). The model takes the following general form for independent variables:
Y =
β
0
+
∑
i = 1
n
β
i
X
i
+
∑
i = 1
n
∑
j = 1
n
β
ij
X
i
X
j
+
∑
i = 1
n
β
ii
X
i
2
where y is the process response or dependent variable; n is the number of the patterns, i and j are the index numbers for the patterns, X1, X2,… Xn, is the independent variable,
β
0
is the free or offset term called the intercept, the
β
i
terms represent the regression coefficient of the model and, βii is the quadratic (squared) effect and βij is the interaction effect in the design point. The number of experimental points (N) can be defined by the following expression:
N = 2 K ( K - 1 ) +
C
0
where K is the number of variables and C0 is the number of center points.

Two kinds of water samples were utilized in this study: tap water and sea water samples. The tap water was collected from the laboratory tap water in Tehran, Iran. The sea water was obtained from Bushehr, the Persian Gulf. The samples were filtered through a 0.45mm pore sized Millipore cellulose nitrate membrane, acidified to pH 1 with HNO3 and stored in a refrigerator in a dark polyethylene bottle until analysis.

Spinach, lettuce and radish samples were purchased from a local supermarket at Tehran in Iran. First, vegetable samples were cleaned with the deionized water. Then, this sample was dried at 110°C. The dried sample was ground to reduce particle size and then thoroughly mixed to ensure the homogeneity of the sample. 1.0g of dried vegetable were put into the beaker, and 10.0mL of concentrated HNO3 and 3.0mL of H2O2 were added, heated until the solution become transparent, and continuously heated to near dryness. The residue was dissolved in 0.1molL−1 HNO3 and then the solution was filtered and diluted with doubly deionized water to 400.0mL in a calibrated flask (Abbasi Tarighat, Mohammadizadeh, & Abdi, 2013).

Nanocomposites based on transition metal oxides (Fe3O4), and carbon nanostructures with low-cost, high activity and good stability been used as an adsorbent for MSPE procedure (Zhao et al., 2015). MNPs, such as Fe3O4 nanoparticles, have several advantages like being produced in large quantity using a simple method, high adsorption capacity because of large surface area, low toxicity and reduce the separation time. However, since the bare Fe3O4 nanoparticles often have poor stability and dispersity, various modification methods have been exploited to get the more dispersible and stable particles. CQDs, as a novel class of nanocarbon, show small particle size (≤10nm), supernal conductivity, rapid electron transfer, high stability, low toxicity, environmental friendliness, favorable biocompatibility, excellent photostability and simple synthetic routes. The abundant functional groups on the surface of the CQDs, especially functional groups containing nitrogen (as in amines groups) as effective components and they can be useful for using in the separation science (Yang et al., 2013, Ju & Chen, 2014). The interaction of hard base N atoms with pair electron in the structure of N-doped CDs and acid Pb+2 ions with empty d orbitals caused the sorbent to be good candidate for lead ions adsorption.

XRD was employed to analyze the crystalline phases of N-doped CDs. The XRD pattern displays a sharp and high-intensity reflection centered at 27.8° that corresponds to the characteristic interplanar stacking peak of aromatic systems, indexed for graphitic materials as the (002) peak (Fig. 1 a) (Li, Kang, Liu, & Lee, 2012). Moreover, the diffraction pattern of the C-dots shows a peak centered at around 24.6°, which is related to the amorphous carbon (Bhattacharya & Deb, 2015; Zhao et al., 2015). The XRD of the N-CQDs after funcionalization of Fe3O4 particles, the characteristic peaks of N-CQDs remain unchanged, while new diffraction peaks were identified at 30.22, 35.67, 43.37, 57.37, and 63.002 (Fig. 1b), corresponding to the (115), (385), (85), (114), and (440) planes of the standard XRD data for Fe3O4, which are in good agreement with the theoretical values. These observations well indicate the formation of Fe3O4/N-CQDs.

The UV–Vis absorption spectrum of N-doped CDs was obtained by dispersing 2mg CDs in 10mL deionized water with sonication, filtering the solution and recording the absorbance data of the remaining solution at 200–800nm. As shown in Fig. S1, the UV–Vis absorption spectrum of aqueous N-doped CDs solution shows an absorption band attributed to the π-π∗ transition of aromatic sp2 domains at 221nm (Dhenadhayalan, Lin, Suresh, & Ramamurthy, 2016; Du et al., 2015).

FT-IR spectra were taken by using KBr to observe the functional groups of Fe3O4/N-CQDs and the spectra were shown in Fig. 2 . As given in Fig. 2a, the main bands of the Fe3O4 NPs at 3414cm−1, 1617cm−1, and 1401cm−1 can be assigned to the OH stretching vibration, CO stretching vibration, and CO bending vibration, respectively; the band at 592cm−1 is attributed to the Fe–O bonds. For the Fe3O4/N-CQDs, the band at 3404cm−1 indicates the existence of OH and NH. The band at 1568cm−1 with a shoulder about 1647cm−1 may be due to the NH bending vibration and the amide CO stretching vibration and the CO (epoxy) stretching vibration peak at 1170cm−1, and the CO (alkoxy) stretching peak at 1072cm−1. The bands at 590cm−1 refer to the FeO bonds in Fe3O4 (Fig. 2b). This result led us to believe that the N-CQDs bound to the Fe3O4 particles through, most probably, FeO chemical bonds. Some other new bands at 1400 and 1170cm−1 may also be attributed to FeO bond formation between the carboxyl groups in N-CQDs and the iron atoms in the Fe3O4 NPs.

The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image of Fe3O4/N-CQDs (Fig. 3 ) shows that they were well separated from each other. Generally, the citrate method leads to core sizes less than 10nm with a rather inhomogeneous size distribution which is similar to that other reported CDs (Bourlinos, Stassinopoulos, Anglos, Zboril, & Karakassides et al., 2008; Bourlinos, Stassinopoulos, Anglos, Zboril, & Georgakilas et al., 2008).

It revealed that sufficient magnetic properties are required for applications of magnetic materials in the aqueous solution. Fig. S2 shows the VSM curve of Fe3O4/N-CQDs at room temperature. The maximum saturation magnetization was 64.52emug−1. The sorbents could be separated from solution with a magnet easily and re-dispersed rapidly as soon as the magnet was taken away due to the superparamagnetic property and large saturation magnetization, which facilitated collection, regeneration, and reutilization of the sorbents.

The adsorption efficiencies of Fe3O4, Fe3O4/C, Fe3O4/N-CQDs sorbents were first investigated. The results are shown in Fig. 4 , which represent significant superiority of Fe3O4/N-CQDs than the other sorbents. The higher sorption degree of Fe3O4/N-CQDs than the other sorbent can be due to their high surface area and excellent dispersing capability in aqueous solutions because of the presence of many polar moieties on the CQD surface.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of effective parameters using central composite design
ce:section.ce:para: Optimization of the extraction procedure was performed at two steps.

The optimization step for the uptake of metal ions on the magnetic sorbent was carried out using FCCCD. Three variables (sample pH, extraction time and amount of the sorbent) were selected as the main factors affecting the sorption step. Other parameters involved in the extraction were kept constant, especially the concentration of lead ions (2mg L−1), and levels of the factors were listed in Table1 S. In this study, totally 17 experiments with 3-factor and 3-center points had to be run for the FCCCD. The relationship between the extraction efficiency of lead ions onto the sorbent and input factors is given in final coded equation as below:
Response = - 6.94078 + ( 4.30967 × pH ) + ( 0.043785 × Extraction time ) + ( 0.097127 × Adsorbent amount ) - ( 0.00452473 × pH × Extraction time ) - ( 0.00764665 × pH × Adsorbent amount ) + ( 0.0000711125 Extraction time × Adsorbent amount ) - ( 0.29037 ×
pH
2
) - ( 0.000164049 ×
Extraction time
2
) - ( 0.000600430 ×
Adsorbent amount
2
) .

Analysis of variance (ANOVA) was performed and it was shown that the model was significant. The significance of each term was determined using the F ratio and P value as presented in Table2 S. The quadratic effects had the most significant (P<0.05) effects on the extraction. The goodness of fit of the model was checked using the determination coefficient (R2). There is a high correlation (R2 =0.9999) between the predicted and actual responses, which indicates that only less than 1% of the total variations are not explained by the model. Also, the “lack of fit” was not significant (p=0.05), which implies that the model fitted the data (Table2S). As seen from p values of sample pH, adsorbent amount (mg) and extraction time are very low; therefore, the main factors have significant effects on the response variables. The use of three-dimensional plots (3D) of the model is highly recommended for graphical interpretation of the interactions. It is helpful to visualize the relationship between the responses and the experimental levels of each factor. Fig. S3. illustrates the relationship between the explanatory and response variables in a three-dimensional representation of the response surface. To this end, one variable was kept at its central level, and the others varied within the experimental range. Fig. S3a shows the interaction between sample pH and adsorbent amount on the response of lead. The results showed that the pH of solution has the largest influence on the removal efficiency and a positive effect upon the extraction. Fig. S3a shows that the extraction efficiencies increase with increasing pH values from 2 to 6.5 and after that it was remained unchanged more or less. Also, Zeta potential measurement, presented in Fig. S4, showed the surface charge of the Fe3O4/N-CQDs particles in different pH values. The point of zero charge of the particles was found to be pH = 5.4 and it confirmed the presence of the anionic group on the surface of Fe3O4/N-CQDs in neutral aqueous solution.

The influence of the adsorbent amount was also evaluated and the results showed that the response of Pb2+ improved with the increase of adsorbent amount from 10 to 25.1 mg and then approximately remained constant. Fig. S3b describes the effect of sample pH and extraction time on the removal percentage of lead ions. The results showed that the quantitative extraction was obtained in the pH of 6.5 and extraction time of 10min. The surface graph, given in Fig. S3c, shows the relative effects of two variables when adsorbent amount is kept constant at central level. The extraction efficiency increases with time rising to 10min. After this time the adsorption process reached to equilibrium. The short time for the equilibrium can be due to the low diffusion resistance of the analyte in the solution to surface of the sorbent. According to the overall results of the optimization study, pH of 6.5, sorption time of 10min, and sorbent amount of 25.1mg were chosen for the sorption step.

One of the quality factors that reduces the number of experimental runs is the type of desorption eluent. Since the adsorption of cations at pH<1 was negligible, it is expected that the elution would be favored in acidic solution. For investigation of eluent’s type, elution of metal ions from the sorbent was studied using 2mL of five solvents such as HCl, H2SO4, HNO3, mixture of HNO3/thiourea and HCl/thiourea. Results showed (Table 3S) that among of three different acid solutions used, 2mL of 0.5molL−1 nitric acid solutions can recover more than 99% of the lead ions from the Fe3O4/N-CQDs, while other acids used were ineffective for complete elution of the ions. Hence, nitric acid solution was chosen as the optimum elution solvent.

In this step, CCD was employed for desorption step while other factors were optimized in the previous section. Eluent concentration (mol L−1), eluent volume (mL), and desorption time (min) were investigated in the elution step using the FCCCD. The following equation explains the relationship of the three variables and extraction recovery of lead in the samples:
Response = 40.20118 + ( 0.36122 × Desorption time ) + ( 116.59564 × Concentration eluent ) + ( 4.34636 × Volume of eluent ) + ( 0.073954 × Desorption time × Concentration eluent ) - ( 2.64029 × Volume of eluent × Concentration eluent ) - ( 64.81549 ×
Concentration eluent
2
) - ( 0.15686 ×
Volume of eluent
2
)

Analysis of Variance (ANOVA) was performed and it was clear that the model was significant. Also, the “lack of fit” was not significant (p=0.05), which gives us the clue that the model fitted to the data. The lack-of-fit measures the failure of the model to represent the data in the experimental domain at points which were not included in the regression. The coefficients of the obtained models are illustrated in Table 4S. The model has the coefficient of determination (adjusted-R2) of 0.9999 that indicates a high degree of correlation between the response and model. RSM graphically illustrate relationships between the parameters and the responses and are the way to obtain an exact optimum value. Fig. S3d, shows the overall response surfaces for the interpretation of the 3D response surface plot, desorption time was kept constant at central level while the other two variables were varied within the experimental ranges. As can be seen in this figure, increasing of eluent concentration and volume enhanced extraction recovery of the target analyte. Also it can be observed from Fig. S3e that the recovery increased as the desorption time and eluent concentration increased. According to the overall results, eluent concentration of 0.5molL−1, elution volume of 1.5mL and elution time of 9min were chosen for desorption of the target ions from the synthesized sorbent.

In the analysis of real samples, the sample volume is one of the important parameters influencing the preconcentration factor. In order to obtain a higher enrichment factor, a large volume of sample solution is required. The effect of sample volume on the adsorption of lead ions on Fe3O4/N-Carbon quantum dots was investigated using different feed volumes of water samples, containing 10µg of lead in each sample in ranging between 50 and 600mL. All solutions were extracted under the optimum condition by the method. The results in Fig. S5 demonstrate that the dilution effect was not significant for sample volumes up to 400mL. Above 400mL, the time required to collect the suspension with a magnet increases. Hence, sample volume of 400mL was selected for the subsequent experiments.

In order to study whether other ions could interfere on the extraction of the target analyte, the procedure was performed in presence of other cations and anions that often accompany with the Pb2+ ions in real-world samples. The recoveries of Pb2+ ions were investigated in the binary mixtures containing lead ions and a foreign ion (Table 5S). It is clear that in excess ratio ([Ion]/[ Pb2+])=1000 of K+, Na+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Cl−, SO4 2−, PO4 3−, NO3 - and 100 of Ag+, Cd2+, Co2+, Cr2+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Hg2+, Ni2+ and Zn2+, these ions did not significant interfere on the extraction efficiency of the analyte. The recoveries of Pb2+ were not less than those in the absence of coexisting ions, indicating that Fe3O4/N-CQDs had a high selectivity towards Pb2+ and present a potential to apply in the real samples.

The capacity of the adsorbent determines how much adsorbent is required for quantitative extraction of a certain amount of metal ions from the solution. In order to determine the adsorption capacity of a sorbent, 25.1mg Fe3O4/N-Carbon quantum dots was added to 400mL of standard solution, with different concentrations of Pb2+ (0.2–40mg L−1), at pH 6.5. After stirring for 20min, the magnetic adsorbent was separated using a magnet, and then the supernatant concentration of Pb2+ was determined by FAAS. The adsorption capacity was calculated as Eq. (1).
(1)
Q
e
=
(
C
0
-
C
e
) × V
m

In Eq. (1), Qe (mg g−1) is the absorbed amounts of lead, and C0 and Ce (mg L−1) are the initial and equilibrium concentrations of lead solution, respectively. V (L) is the volume of the initial solution, and m (g) is the mass of adsorbent. The results were shown in Fig. S6. The adsorption capacity drastically increases at low initial concentrations and then continues to accelerate with a slower rate at higher concentrations until a state of equilibrium is attained. To gain a better understanding of the mechanism of lead removal, two classical adsorption models, Langmuir and Freundlich isotherm models, were applied to analyse adsorption data. The linear form of the Langmuir isotherm can be expressed by the Eq. (2).
(2)
C
e
q
e
=
1
θ b
+
C
e
θ
where qe (mg g−1) and Ce (mg L−1) were the equilibrium adsorption capacity and the equilibrium concentration,
θ
was the maximum adsorption capacity and b was the equilibrium adsorption constant. The maximum adsorption capacity
θ
could be calculated from the slope of the linear plot of Ce/qe versus Ce.

The linear form of Freundlich isotherm model can be expressed by the following equation.
(3)
ln
q
e
= ln
K
F
+
1
n
ln
C
e
where KF (mg−1/
n L1/
n g−1) and n were the Freundlich constants. The value of n and KF could be obtained from slope of linear plot. The Langmuir and Freundlich isotherm constants for Pb2+ adsorption on Fe3O4/N-CQDs are shown in Table 6S. The results show that the adsorption process fits well with the Langmuir model due to the high correlation coefficient (R2 =9949). Therefore, the maximum adsorption capacity of lead ions was found to be 303.03 mg per gram of Fe3O4/N-CQDs adsorbent.

The Langmuir isotherm model is valid for monolayer adsorption onto a surface containing a finite number of identical sorption sites and there is no important interaction between the adsorbate molecules adsorbed on the adjacent adsorption sites of the adsorbent. It can be concluded that the chemisorption of the analyte can occur on the surface of the sorbent.

To evaluate the possibility of reusing and regenerating the sorbent, reusability tests were performed by several consecutive adsorption/desorption cycles under optimum conditions. It was found that the degree of sorption of the adsorbent after 10 cycles of equilibration with the metal ion displays no significant changes. Therefore, the repeated use of the adsorbent is feasible as shown in Fig. S7. The results indicated that the adsorption sites were not destroyed by the desorption solution.

The analytical characteristics of the method, i.e., precision, detection limits and linearity of Pb2+ determination in aqueous samples were investigated based on the adsorption of Pb2+ on Fe3O4/N-CQDs under the optimum conditions. A good linearity was observed over the concentration range of 0.3–300µgL−1 with the equation of y=16.891 x+0.0465 and correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.9992, where y and x are absorbance and concentration of Pb2+ in µgL−1, respectively. The limit of detection (LOD) which defined as 3S/m (where S is the standard deviation of three replicates of blank signals and m is the slope of the calibration curve after preconcentration), was found to be (0.082) µgL−1. The limit of quantitation was also 0.3μgL−1. The preconcentration factors (PFs), defined as the ratio of the calibration curve slope obtained before and after preconcentration were found to be 265 for lead ions. The precision for six replicate determinations at 0.6µgL−1 of Pb2+ was 1.9% as relative standard deviation (R.S.D.).

A comparison of the introduced method with the other reported methods for extraction and determination of lead ions is presented in Table 1. It can be seen that some obtained values for the introduced method, such as LOD, linear range and enrichment factor, are as good as, or better than, some previously reported methods. Furthermore, the introduced procedure shows much larger adsorption capacity over other methods and higher speed analysis than the conventional SPE methods.

The method was used for the determination of Pb2+ in several environmental water and vegetable samples. To examine the reliability and accuracy of the method, different amounts of the metal ions were spiked into the samples at optimum conditions. The recovery of spiked samples is satisfactorily reasonable and was confirmed using addition method, which indicates the capability of the system in the determination of ions in real matrices. The recovery values calculated for the added standards were higher than 97%, thus confirming the accuracy of the procedure and its independence from the matrix effects (Table 2).

A facile approach for the synthesis of Fe3O4/N-CQDs composite through co-precipitation was developed and its application for the extraction of lead ions from aqueous solutions was explored. The developed method is simple, rapid and low cost. The good analytical features of the method showed that it is an appropriate and promised one. The method provides good precision and high accuracy indicated by the quantitative recovery of the spiked Pb2+ samples. Other advantages of this method are: low time-consumption due to the magnetically-assisted separation of the sorbent and higher surface area; therefore, satisfactory results can be achieved by using fewer amounts of the sorbent. Due to the relatively high preconcentration factor, trace amounts of heavy metals at ngmL−1 levels in high-volume samples can be quantified by the magnetic sorbent.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2017.07.042.

ce:e-component.ce:label: Supplementary Tables S1–S6 and Figs. S1–S7
